description: A systematic framework for planning, executing, and documenting analytical method validation to ensure regulatory compliance, data integrity, and fitness for purpose.
principles: Define validation scope and acceptance criteria before experimentation begins.
Demonstrate specificity and accuracy to prove the method isolates the target analyte.
Establish precision and linearity across the full intended concentration range.
Verify robustness by testing deliberate, small variations in method parameters.
Run system suitability checks before and after every analytical sequence.
Document all raw data, deviations, and calculations with full traceability.
Treat validation as a lifecycle activity, not a one-time compliance checkbox.

workflow.purpose: Establish scope, regulatory alignment, and measurable success criteria.
guidelines: Map the intended method use to required ICH parameters.
Draft a formal validation protocol with clear acceptance thresholds.
Identify reference standards, matrices, and equipment requirements.
Secure stakeholder approval before any testing begins.
pitfalls: Setting acceptance criteria after data collection.
Ignoring regulatory context or intended use case.
Vague scope that leaves critical parameters undefined.

workflow.purpose: Structure experiments to efficiently cover all validation parameters.
guidelines: Use orthogonal or factorial designs to minimize runs while maximizing coverage.
Prepare certified reference materials and spiked matrices for accuracy checks.
Plan system suitability tests to monitor instrument stability.
Allocate sufficient replicates for statistical confidence.
pitfalls: Overlapping experiments without clear parameter separation.
Insufficient replicates leading to unreliable statistics.

workflow.purpose: Generate reliable, reproducible data under controlled conditions.
guidelines: Follow the approved protocol without unapproved deviations.
Log all deviations, environmental changes, or instrument anomalies immediately.
Run system suitability checks before and after sample sequences.
Maintain strict chain of custody for all samples and standards.
pitfalls: Skipping system suitability to save time.
Adjusting parameters mid-run to force acceptable results.

workflow.purpose: Statistically assess results against predefined criteria.
guidelines: Apply appropriate statistical tests for each parameter (e.g., ANOVA, regression).
Calculate confidence intervals and review residual plots for bias.
Document pass/fail status for every validation parameter.
Investigate outliers rigorously before exclusion.
pitfalls: Misapplying statistical methods to non-linear or non-normal data.
Conflating precision with accuracy or specificity.

workflow.purpose: Create an auditable, regulatory-ready validation summary.
guidelines: Structure the report according to ICH or internal SOP templates.
Include direct references to raw data, calculations, and protocol versions.
State clear conclusions and fitness-for-purpose statements.
Archive all documents with version control and access restrictions.
pitfalls: Incomplete traceability between results and raw data.
Omitting negative results or failed parameters.
Vague conclusions that leave regulatory reviewers uncertain.

checklist: Validation protocol approved and signed before experimentation.
All acceptance criteria defined and statistically justified.
System suitability criteria met for every analytical sequence.
Specificity and accuracy demonstrated with blank and spiked matrices.
Linearity range covers intended concentrations with validated statistics.
Robustness tested via deliberate parameter variations.
All raw data, calculations, and deviations fully archived.
Validation report reviewed, approved, and version-controlled.

source.book: Method Validation in Pharmaceutical Analysis - A Guide to Best Practice
source.author: Joachim Ermer, John H. McB. Miller (eds.)

vocabulary.definition: International guideline defining validation parameters, statistical approaches, and acceptance criteria for analytical methods.
Pre-run checks ensuring the analytical system performs within specified limits before sample analysis begins.
Measure of a method's capacity to remain unaffected by small, deliberate variations in experimental parameters.
Limit of Detection and Limit of Quantification; lowest concentrations reliably detected or quantified with acceptable precision.
Closeness of test results to the true value, typically assessed via recovery studies using spiked samples.

heuristics: Define criteria before data exists; never adjust targets to fit results.
Validate the entire workflow, not just the instrument or software.
If a parameter cannot be statistically justified, the method is not fit for purpose.
